[metadata] {"type": "text", "file": {"filePath": "/app/papers/PMC5189722.md", "content": "# Effect of the Polymorphism of Folylpolyglutamate Synthetase on Treatment of High-Dose Methotrexate in Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia\n\n## Metadata\n**Authors:** Zhen Huang, Hong-Fei Tong, Yuan Li, Jiang-Chao Qian, Ju-Xiang Wang, Zhe Wang, Ji-Chen Ruan\n**Journal:** Medical Science Monitor : International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research\n**Date:** 2016 Dec 17\n**DOI:** [10.12659/MSM.899021](https://doi.org/10.12659/MSM.899021)\n**PMID:** 27987364\n**PMCID:** PMC5189722\n**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5189722/\n**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf)\n\n## Abstract\n\n**Background:** \nThe aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.\n\n**Material/Methods:** \n57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.\n\n**Results:** \nNo differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.\n\n**Conclusions:** \nPolymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.\n\nMeSH Keywords: gamma-Glutamyl Hydrolase; Polymorphism, Single Nucleotide; Precursor Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia-Lymphoma\n\n### Background\n\nThe aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.\n\n### Material/Methods\n\n57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.\n\n### Results\n\nNo differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.\n\n### Conclusions\n\nPolymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.\n\n## Background\n\nAcute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is one of the most common hematologic malignancies in children. High-dose methotrexate (HD-MTX) has been shown to play an important role in the prophylaxis and treatment of extramedullary leukemia [[1](#b1-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Different treatment efficiency and adverse effects were observed in different individuals even with the same dose of MTX, suggesting the existence of differences in the MTX metabolism *in vivo* [[2](#b2-medscimonit-22-4967)]. As a catalase, folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) plays a critical role in the metabolism of MTX, and its deficiency can cause a dramatic reduction of intracellular MTX polyglutamate (MTXPG) level, leading to MTX resistance in leukemia cells [[3](#b3-medscimonit-22-4967),[4](#b4-medscimonit-22-4967)], which are further supported by the increased sensitivity of several glioma cells to MTX after overexpression of FPGS [[5](#b5-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In addition, a correlation of FPGS expression with intracellular MTXPG level and treatment outcome has been observed in pediatric patients with ALL [[6](#b6-medscimonit-22-4967),[7](#b7-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS rs1544105, resulting from C to T, has been identified as a functional polymorphism affecting FPGS activity in pediatric patients with ALL [[8](#b8-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Considering the important role of FPGS playing in MTX metabolism, whether FPGS’s function polymorphism rs1544105 affects the treatment of MTX in pediatric patients with ALL remains poorly understood. In this study, we aimed to analyze the genotype of rs1544105 in pediatric ALL patients and to conduct a preliminary investigation of the relationship between rs1544105 genotype with MTX plasma concentration or ALL prognosis.\n\n## Material and Methods\n\n### Patients\n\nFrom July 2012 to October 2014, 57 pediatric patients with newly diagnosed ALL were included in this study. The diagnosis of ALL was based on international guidelines [[9](#b9-medscimonit-22-4967)] and further confirmed by bone marrow cytology. All patients achieved remission after treatment with VDLP (Vincristine, Daunorubicin, L-asparaginase, and Prednisone). High-dose methotrexate was used for the prophylaxis of lymphocytic leukemia. Those patients were excluded from this study, including those who left the hospital or died before the end of 1 course of treatment, had impaired function of liver or kidney before chemotherapy, or who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Thirty-one age and sex-matched healthy children were served as a control group. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.\n\n### Reagents\n\nMethotrexate (batch no. HY21A) was purchased from Ruihui Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Reference substance for methotrexate (batch no. HWG00526) and levofloxacin (batch no. 130537-200301) were from the Chinese Institute for Drug Control.\n\n### Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism (PCR-RFLP)\n\nGenomic DNA was extracted from patients or control followed by PCR-RFLP analysis of the distribution of rs1544105 genotype, as previously described [[10](#b10-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Briefly, PCR amplification was performed with the following conditions: Initial denaturation (95°C for 10 min), 40 cycles of denaturation (95°C for 15 s), annealing (60°C for 1 min) and extension (72°C for 1 min), and final extension (72°C for 7 min). Sequences for PCR primers were: Forward: 5′-CCCAGAGTCCTTATTCTTAGCC-3′ and Reverse: 5′-GTGCCTCCTTCACACACAG-3′. After completion of amplification, restriction enzyme HpyCH4IV was added into the PCR product and incubated at 37°C for 4 h, followed by 2.5% agarose gel electrophoresis for analysis of RFLP.\n\n### Measurement of MTX concentration\n\nPeripheral venous blood was drawn from patients at 24 h or 44 h after MTX treatment, followed by measurement of MTX concentration by HPLC, as described previously [[11](#b11-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The conditions used for chromatographic analysis were as follows: stationary phase [ZORBAX XDB-C18 (4.6×250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA)], guard column [XDB-C18 (4.6×12.5 mm, 5 μm)], mobile phase [methyl cyanide /H_2_O: 30/70 (V/V), rate: 1 ml/min, column temperature: 30°C]. The detection wavelength was 225 nm.\n\n### Statistical analysis\n\nSPSS 19.0 software was used for statistical analysis. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium of genotype distribution as well as the difference of genotype in sex and risk group were evaluated by chi-square test. Comparison of MTX concentration within different genotypes was performed by one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test. Overall survival was assessed by Kaplan-Meier method. P<0.05 was considered to be a statistically significant difference.\n\n## Results\n\n### Patients’ characteristics\n\nThis study included 57 pediatric ALL patients and 31 healthy children. No significant differences were observed in these 2 groups in sex or age. Detailed information is listed in [Table 1](#t1-medscimonit-22-4967).\n\n### Table 1.\n\n| Parameters | ALL group | The control group |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Age(years) | 5.9±4.3 | 3.7±1.6 |\n| Gender(n) |  |  |\n| Male | 31 | 18 |\n| Female | 26 | 13 |\n| ALL (n) |  |  |\n| T-Lineage | 11 |   |\n| B-Lineage | 46 |   |\n| Risk (n) |  |  |\n| Standard | 21 |   |\n| Intermediate | 27 |   |\n| High | 9 |   |\n| WBC (109 L) |  |  |\n| <50 | 52 |   |\n| ≥50 | 5 |   |\n\nTable 1 Caption: Clinical characteristics of patients with ALL and the control group.\n\n### Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105\n\nPolymorphism of rs1544105 was evaluated using the PCR-RFLP approach. After PCR amplification of FPGS, restricted enzyme HpyCH4IV was used to detect the mutation of rs1544105. After HpyCH4IV treatment on PCR product, 3 genotypes were detected based on the length after electrophoresis: homozygotes AA (298 bp), heterozygotes GA (298 bp, 168 bp, and 130 bp), and homozygotes GG (168 bp and 130 bp) ([Figure 1](#f1-medscimonit-22-4967)).\n\n### Figure 1.\n\n![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad94ef6b2a44/medscimonit-22-4967-g001.jpg)\n\nElectrophoresis of PCR-RFLP product. Genomic DNA was isolated from patients or controls, followed by analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 by PCR-RFLP. Lane 1 and 2: GA, Lane 3: GG, Lane 4: Marker, Lane 5 and 6: AA.\n\n### Genotype and alleles of FPGF rs1544105\n\nThe genotype distribution of FPGS rs1544105 in ALL patients and healthy controls was in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (P>0.05). The distribution frequency of alleles was similar in these 2 groups. Compared to allele A, odds ratio (OR) of allele G was 0.97, with no significant difference ([Table 2](#t2-medscimonit-22-4967)).\n\n### Table 2.\n\n|   | Genotype (%) |  |  | Gene frequency |  | OR | 95%CI |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| GG | GA | AA | G | A |  |  |  |\n| ALL (n=57) | 11 (19.30%) | 21 (36.84%) | 25 (43.86%) | 43 (37.72%) | 71 (62.28%) | 0.965 | 0.764–1.219 |\n| Control (n=31) | 4 (12.90%) | 14 (45.16%) | 13 (41.94%) | 22 (35.48%) | 40 (64.52%) | 1 |   |\n| P | 0.654a |  |  | 0.769b |  |   |   |\n\nTable 2 Caption: Distribution of FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism in ALL and control.\n\n### Chromatographic analysis of MTX\n\nChromatomaps for blank plasma ([Figure 2A](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), MTX plasma standard ([Figure 2B](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), and patient plasma ([Figure 2C](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)) were obtained through HPLC analysis ([Figure 2](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)). Chromatographic peak of MTX could be separated completely without obvious interference of endogenous impurity peak. The retention time for MTX and levofloxacin (internal standard) was 4.92 min and 9.42 min, respectively. A significant linear relation between MTX concentration and the ratio of MTX to internal standard peak area (Ai/As) was observed with regression equation being C=6.1328Ai/As – 0.0195 (r=0.9999) when MTX concentration was in 0.03–5.00 μg/ml, which was obtained from the standard curve under the current condition in this study. The lowest detected concentration was 0.03 μg/ml (RS, N=3). The recovery rate was 100.317±2.401%. Intra- and inter-batch errors as measured by precision test were 5.88% and 6.50%, respectively. MTX plasma samples were stable regardless of the storage condition, such as room temperature, frozen preservation, or freeze-thaw condition.\n\n### Figure 2.\n\n![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/13a08c3247ee/medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)\n\nHPLC analysis of the plasma concentration of MTX. Through using blank plasma (A) and MTX plasma standard (B), plasma concentration of MTX (C) after administration was measured by HPLC.\n\n### Relationship between polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 and MTX concentration\n\nIn this study, we used dose-corrected MTX concentration per body surface area as an indicator to investigate the effect of polymorphism on MTX concentration. Our results showed that MTX C/D value (at 24 h) was significantly higher in ALL patients with AA genotype of rs1544105 than in patients with other genotypes (P<0.05) ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)). However, no difference in MTX C/D value was observed within different genotypes at 44 h ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)).\n\n### Table 3.\n\n| Time after MTX infusion | FPGS genotype | C/D (mmol/L per g/m2) | F | P |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| 24 hour | AA | 15.30±2.98 | 4.125 | 0.023a |\n| AG | 14.14±3.19 |  |  |  |\n| GG | 12.19±2.59 |  |  |  |\n| 44 hour | AA | 0.11±0.04 | 3.065 | 0.055b |\n| AG | 0.11±0.05 |  |  |  |\n| GG | 0.08±0.03 |  |  |  |\n\nTable 3 Caption: Relationship between FPGS genotype and concentration of MTX.\n\n### Association of rs1544105 polymorphism with prognosis\n\nThe follow-up period in this study was from July 2012 to October 2014. Kaplan-Meier method was used for analysis of the association of rs1544105 polymorphism with survival time. Our results revealed a significant difference of overall survival between patients with AA genotype and GA + GG genotype (P<0.05) ([Table 4](#t4-medscimonit-22-4967)). After adjustment of white blood cell count, sex, age, and immune typing by Cox multivariate analysis, FPGS rs1544105 alleles were demonstrated to be an indicator for poor prognosis (log rank P=0.013, hazard ratio: 0.447, 95%CI: 0.237–0.842) ([Figure 3](#f3-medscimonit-22-4967)).\n\n### Table 4.\n\n| Group | Median survival time (w) |  | 95% Confidence Interval (w) |  | F | P |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Estimate | Std Error | Lower bound | Upper bound |  |  |  |\n| Overall | 37.400 | 6.912 | 23.853 | 50.947 |   |   |\n| AA | 52.000 | 12.079 | 28.326 | 75.674 |   |   |\n| AG+GG | 35.000 | 4.011 | 27.139 | 42.861 | 4.352 | 0.037a |\n\nTable 4 Caption: Overall survival analysis in ALL patients with different FPGS genotypes.\n\n### Figure 3.\n\n![Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad233f47ed4e/medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)\n\nAnalysis of overall survival and Cox regression in ALL patients with different genotypes. Overall survival in patients treated with MTX was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.\n\n## Discussion\n\nAs an anti-tumor drug in the chemotherapy of pediatric ALL patients, methotrexate (MTX) plays an important role in the consolidation and maintenance treatment. In the clinic, plasma concentration of MTX and release of calcium folinate are monitored dynamically to reduce the occurrence of chemotherapy-induced adverse effects. A previous study demonstrated the existence of individual differences in the pharmacokinetics, effectiveness, and toxicity of MTX, suggesting there might be some potential gene targets affecting the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of MTX [[12](#b12-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS is an important enzyme in MTX metabolism, and catalyzed poly glutamic acid is the core of folate antagonists-associated cytotoxic therapy [[13](#b13-medscimonit-22-4967),[14](#b14-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Therefore, investigating the effect of FPGS polymorphism on drug treatment in ALL patients would have some important guiding significance [[15](#b15-medscimonit-22-4967)].\n\nThrough analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105, we found that the genotype distribution in ALL patients and healthy controls were in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. The distribution frequencies of GG, GA, and AA genotype in healthy controls were close to HAPMap-HCB (Han Chinese in Beijing, China), which was 11.6%, 39.5%, and 48.8%, respectively [[16](#b16-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In this study we found the distribution frequency of alleles in ALL patients was close to that in controls, indicating no significant correlation of FPGS polymorphism with the pathogenesis of ALL, consistent with a previous study [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)].\n\nAccording to recommendations for ALL diagnosis and treatment, MTX doses for patients with low, medium, or high risk are different. To exclude the interference of different doses, dose-standardized MTX plasma concentration was used as an analysis indicator to monitor the dynamical concentration of MTX. Our results showed a significant difference in MTX concentration among patients with different genotypes at 24 h, showing a higher concentration for AA genotype than GA and GC (P<0.05). However, no difference was observed at 44 h. There are few reports on the relationship between FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism and the sensitivity and toxicity of MTX-associated chemotherapy, with contradicting results. Liu et al. [[18](#b18-medscimonit-22-4967)] and Panetta et al. [[19](#b19-medscimonit-22-4967)] demonstrated that FPGS activity was higher in individuals with GG than other genotypes with higher MTX plasma concentration as well as increased sensitivity to MTX. Sharma et al [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)]showed allele G might reduce the MTX treatment efficiency. In our study a difference of MTX concentration was found within different genotypes at 24 h but not 44 h. There may be several reasons to explain this. First, MTX metabolism is regulated by multiple genes, such as methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase, and γ-glutamyltransferase [[20](#b20-medscimonit-22-4967),[21](#b21-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The exact role of each gene in MTX metabolism is not clear, and the interaction of each gene with FPGS makes it difficult to interpret the results we obtained. Second, a single time point was chosen in this study to reflect the system exposure extent of the drug, which might have had a large influence on the result precision when the plasma concentration was lower, which might be why no difference was found at 44 h. Third, the Kruskal-Wallis test was performed on the samples at 44 h because they were not in normal distribution (P=0.032) and the P value was equal to 0.055. Therefore, a large sample-size study was required to confirm the finding.\n\nIn this study we found higher efficacy of chemotherapy in patients with AA genotype than in those with GA and GG, further supported by the overall survival. The median survival time of patients with AA was significantly longer than in patients with other genotypes, with a significant difference of overall survival. Regarding the studies on MTX resistance, Stark et al. [[22](#b22-medscimonit-22-4967)] found impaired activity of FPGS in patients with MTX resistance. Further study in these patients demonstrated a defect in FPGS mRNA splicing, leading to premature termination and subsequent FPGS dysfunction and drug resistance. However, the exact mechanism by which this polymorphism affects FPGS function is not clear and requires further investigation.\n\n## Conclusions\n\nOur study demonstrated a correlation of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 with MTX treatment efficacy, as well as with overall survival, suggesting it could be used as an effective approach in the prediction of MTX treatment efficacy.\n\n## Footnotes\n\n## References\n\n1. # Effect of the Polymorphism of Folylpolyglutamate Synthetase on Treatment of High-Dose Methotrexate in Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia

## Metadata
**Authors:** Zhen Huang, Hong-Fei Tong, Yuan Li, Jiang-Chao Qian, Ju-Xiang Wang, Zhe Wang, Ji-Chen Ruan
**Journal:** Medical Science Monitor : International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
**Date:** 2016 Dec 17
**DOI:** [10.12659/MSM.899021](https://doi.org/10.12659/MSM.899021)
**PMID:** 27987364
**PMCID:** PMC5189722
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5189722/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf)

## Abstract

**Background:** 
The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.

**Material/Methods:** 
57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

**Results:** 
No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.

**Conclusions:** 
Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.

MeSH Keywords: gamma-Glutamyl Hydrolase; Polymorphism, Single Nucleotide; Precursor Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia-Lymphoma

### Background

The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.

### Material/Methods

57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

### Results

No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.

### Conclusions

Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.

## Background

Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is one of the most common hematologic malignancies in children. High-dose methotrexate (HD-MTX) has been shown to play an important role in the prophylaxis and treatment of extramedullary leukemia [[1](#b1-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Different treatment efficiency and adverse effects were observed in different individuals even with the same dose of MTX, suggesting the existence of differences in the MTX metabolism *in vivo* [[2](#b2-medscimonit-22-4967)]. As a catalase, folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) plays a critical role in the metabolism of MTX, and its deficiency can cause a dramatic reduction of intracellular MTX polyglutamate (MTXPG) level, leading to MTX resistance in leukemia cells [[3](#b3-medscimonit-22-4967),[4](#b4-medscimonit-22-4967)], which are further supported by the increased sensitivity of several glioma cells to MTX after overexpression of FPGS [[5](#b5-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In addition, a correlation of FPGS expression with intracellular MTXPG level and treatment outcome has been observed in pediatric patients with ALL [[6](#b6-medscimonit-22-4967),[7](#b7-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS rs1544105, resulting from C to T, has been identified as a functional polymorphism affecting FPGS activity in pediatric patients with ALL [[8](#b8-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Considering the important role of FPGS playing in MTX metabolism, whether FPGS’s function polymorphism rs1544105 affects the treatment of MTX in pediatric patients with ALL remains poorly understood. In this study, we aimed to analyze the genotype of rs1544105 in pediatric ALL patients and to conduct a preliminary investigation of the relationship between rs1544105 genotype with MTX plasma concentration or ALL prognosis.

## Material and Methods

### Patients

From July 2012 to October 2014, 57 pediatric patients with newly diagnosed ALL were included in this study. The diagnosis of ALL was based on international guidelines [[9](#b9-medscimonit-22-4967)] and further confirmed by bone marrow cytology. All patients achieved remission after treatment with VDLP (Vincristine, Daunorubicin, L-asparaginase, and Prednisone). High-dose methotrexate was used for the prophylaxis of lymphocytic leukemia. Those patients were excluded from this study, including those who left the hospital or died before the end of 1 course of treatment, had impaired function of liver or kidney before chemotherapy, or who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Thirty-one age and sex-matched healthy children were served as a control group. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.

### Reagents

Methotrexate (batch no. HY21A) was purchased from Ruihui Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Reference substance for methotrexate (batch no. HWG00526) and levofloxacin (batch no. 130537-200301) were from the Chinese Institute for Drug Control.

### Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism (PCR-RFLP)

Genomic DNA was extracted from patients or control followed by PCR-RFLP analysis of the distribution of rs1544105 genotype, as previously described [[10](#b10-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Briefly, PCR amplification was performed with the following conditions: Initial denaturation (95°C for 10 min), 40 cycles of denaturation (95°C for 15 s), annealing (60°C for 1 min) and extension (72°C for 1 min), and final extension (72°C for 7 min). Sequences for PCR primers were: Forward: 5′-CCCAGAGTCCTTATTCTTAGCC-3′ and Reverse: 5′-GTGCCTCCTTCACACACAG-3′. After completion of amplification, restriction enzyme HpyCH4IV was added into the PCR product and incubated at 37°C for 4 h, followed by 2.5% agarose gel electrophoresis for analysis of RFLP.

### Measurement of MTX concentration

Peripheral venous blood was drawn from patients at 24 h or 44 h after MTX treatment, followed by measurement of MTX concentration by HPLC, as described previously [[11](#b11-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The conditions used for chromatographic analysis were as follows: stationary phase [ZORBAX XDB-C18 (4.6×250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA)], guard column [XDB-C18 (4.6×12.5 mm, 5 μm)], mobile phase [methyl cyanide /H_2_O: 30/70 (V/V), rate: 1 ml/min, column temperature: 30°C]. The detection wavelength was 225 nm.

### Statistical analysis

SPSS 19.0 software was used for statistical analysis. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium of genotype distribution as well as the difference of genotype in sex and risk group were evaluated by chi-square test. Comparison of MTX concentration within different genotypes was performed by one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test. Overall survival was assessed by Kaplan-Meier method. P<0.05 was considered to be a statistically significant difference.

## Results

### Patients’ characteristics

This study included 57 pediatric ALL patients and 31 healthy children. No significant differences were observed in these 2 groups in sex or age. Detailed information is listed in [Table 1](#t1-medscimonit-22-4967).

### Table 1.

| Parameters | ALL group | The control group |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Age(years) | 5.9±4.3 | 3.7±1.6 |
| Gender(n) |  |  |
| Male | 31 | 18 |
| Female | 26 | 13 |
| ALL (n) |  |  |
| T-Lineage | 11 |   |
| B-Lineage | 46 |   |
| Risk (n) |  |  |
| Standard | 21 |   |
| Intermediate | 27 |   |
| High | 9 |   |
| WBC (109 L) |  |  |
| <50 | 52 |   |
| ≥50 | 5 |   |

Table 1 Caption: Clinical characteristics of patients with ALL and the control group.

### Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105

Polymorphism of rs1544105 was evaluated using the PCR-RFLP approach. After PCR amplification of FPGS, restricted enzyme HpyCH4IV was used to detect the mutation of rs1544105. After HpyCH4IV treatment on PCR product, 3 genotypes were detected based on the length after electrophoresis: homozygotes AA (298 bp), heterozygotes GA (298 bp, 168 bp, and 130 bp), and homozygotes GG (168 bp and 130 bp) ([Figure 1](#f1-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Figure 1.

![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad94ef6b2a44/medscimonit-22-4967-g001.jpg)

Electrophoresis of PCR-RFLP product. Genomic DNA was isolated from patients or controls, followed by analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 by PCR-RFLP. Lane 1 and 2: GA, Lane 3: GG, Lane 4: Marker, Lane 5 and 6: AA.

### Genotype and alleles of FPGF rs1544105

The genotype distribution of FPGS rs1544105 in ALL patients and healthy controls was in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (P>0.05). The distribution frequency of alleles was similar in these 2 groups. Compared to allele A, odds ratio (OR) of allele G was 0.97, with no significant difference ([Table 2](#t2-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 2.

|   | Genotype (%) |  |  | Gene frequency |  | OR | 95%CI |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| GG | GA | AA | G | A |  |  |  |
| ALL (n=57) | 11 (19.30%) | 21 (36.84%) | 25 (43.86%) | 43 (37.72%) | 71 (62.28%) | 0.965 | 0.764–1.219 |
| Control (n=31) | 4 (12.90%) | 14 (45.16%) | 13 (41.94%) | 22 (35.48%) | 40 (64.52%) | 1 |   |
| P | 0.654a |  |  | 0.769b |  |   |   |

Table 2 Caption: Distribution of FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism in ALL and control.

### Chromatographic analysis of MTX

Chromatomaps for blank plasma ([Figure 2A](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), MTX plasma standard ([Figure 2B](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), and patient plasma ([Figure 2C](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)) were obtained through HPLC analysis ([Figure 2](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)). Chromatographic peak of MTX could be separated completely without obvious interference of endogenous impurity peak. The retention time for MTX and levofloxacin (internal standard) was 4.92 min and 9.42 min, respectively. A significant linear relation between MTX concentration and the ratio of MTX to internal standard peak area (Ai/As) was observed with regression equation being C=6.1328Ai/As – 0.0195 (r=0.9999) when MTX concentration was in 0.03–5.00 μg/ml, which was obtained from the standard curve under the current condition in this study. The lowest detected concentration was 0.03 μg/ml (RS, N=3). The recovery rate was 100.317±2.401%. Intra- and inter-batch errors as measured by precision test were 5.88% and 6.50%, respectively. MTX plasma samples were stable regardless of the storage condition, such as room temperature, frozen preservation, or freeze-thaw condition.

### Figure 2.

![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/13a08c3247ee/medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)

HPLC analysis of the plasma concentration of MTX. Through using blank plasma (A) and MTX plasma standard (B), plasma concentration of MTX (C) after administration was measured by HPLC.

### Relationship between polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 and MTX concentration

In this study, we used dose-corrected MTX concentration per body surface area as an indicator to investigate the effect of polymorphism on MTX concentration. Our results showed that MTX C/D value (at 24 h) was significantly higher in ALL patients with AA genotype of rs1544105 than in patients with other genotypes (P<0.05) ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)). However, no difference in MTX C/D value was observed within different genotypes at 44 h ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 3.

| Time after MTX infusion | FPGS genotype | C/D (mmol/L per g/m2) | F | P |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 24 hour | AA | 15.30±2.98 | 4.125 | 0.023a |
| AG | 14.14±3.19 |  |  |  |
| GG | 12.19±2.59 |  |  |  |
| 44 hour | AA | 0.11±0.04 | 3.065 | 0.055b |
| AG | 0.11±0.05 |  |  |  |
| GG | 0.08±0.03 |  |  |  |

Table 3 Caption: Relationship between FPGS genotype and concentration of MTX.

### Association of rs1544105 polymorphism with prognosis

The follow-up period in this study was from July 2012 to October 2014. Kaplan-Meier method was used for analysis of the association of rs1544105 polymorphism with survival time. Our results revealed a significant difference of overall survival between patients with AA genotype and GA + GG genotype (P<0.05) ([Table 4](#t4-medscimonit-22-4967)). After adjustment of white blood cell count, sex, age, and immune typing by Cox multivariate analysis, FPGS rs1544105 alleles were demonstrated to be an indicator for poor prognosis (log rank P=0.013, hazard ratio: 0.447, 95%CI: 0.237–0.842) ([Figure 3](#f3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 4.

| Group | Median survival time (w) |  | 95% Confidence Interval (w) |  | F | P |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Estimate | Std Error | Lower bound | Upper bound |  |  |  |
| Overall | 37.400 | 6.912 | 23.853 | 50.947 |   |   |
| AA | 52.000 | 12.079 | 28.326 | 75.674 |   |   |
| AG+GG | 35.000 | 4.011 | 27.139 | 42.861 | 4.352 | 0.037a |

Table 4 Caption: Overall survival analysis in ALL patients with different FPGS genotypes.

### Figure 3.

![Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad233f47ed4e/medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)

Analysis of overall survival and Cox regression in ALL patients with different genotypes. Overall survival in patients treated with MTX was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

## Discussion

As an anti-tumor drug in the chemotherapy of pediatric ALL patients, methotrexate (MTX) plays an important role in the consolidation and maintenance treatment. In the clinic, plasma concentration of MTX and release of calcium folinate are monitored dynamically to reduce the occurrence of chemotherapy-induced adverse effects. A previous study demonstrated the existence of individual differences in the pharmacokinetics, effectiveness, and toxicity of MTX, suggesting there might be some potential gene targets affecting the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of MTX [[12](#b12-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS is an important enzyme in MTX metabolism, and catalyzed poly glutamic acid is the core of folate antagonists-associated cytotoxic therapy [[13](#b13-medscimonit-22-4967),[14](#b14-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Therefore, investigating the effect of FPGS polymorphism on drug treatment in ALL patients would have some important guiding significance [[15](#b15-medscimonit-22-4967)].

Through analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105, we found that the genotype distribution in ALL patients and healthy controls were in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. The distribution frequencies of GG, GA, and AA genotype in healthy controls were close to HAPMap-HCB (Han Chinese in Beijing, China), which was 11.6%, 39.5%, and 48.8%, respectively [[16](#b16-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In this study we found the distribution frequency of alleles in ALL patients was close to that in controls, indicating no significant correlation of FPGS polymorphism with the pathogenesis of ALL, consistent with a previous study [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)].

According to recommendations for ALL diagnosis and treatment, MTX doses for patients with low, medium, or high risk are different. To exclude the interference of different doses, dose-standardized MTX plasma concentration was used as an analysis indicator to monitor the dynamical concentration of MTX. Our results showed a significant difference in MTX concentration among patients with different genotypes at 24 h, showing a higher concentration for AA genotype than GA and GC (P<0.05). However, no difference was observed at 44 h. There are few reports on the relationship between FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism and the sensitivity and toxicity of MTX-associated chemotherapy, with contradicting results. Liu et al. [[18](#b18-medscimonit-22-4967)] and Panetta et al. [[19](#b19-medscimonit-22-4967)] demonstrated that FPGS activity was higher in individuals with GG than other genotypes with higher MTX plasma concentration as well as increased sensitivity to MTX. Sharma et al [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)]showed allele G might reduce the MTX treatment efficiency. In our study a difference of MTX concentration was found within different genotypes at 24 h but not 44 h. There may be several reasons to explain this. First, MTX metabolism is regulated by multiple genes, such as methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase, and γ-glutamyltransferase [[20](#b20-medscimonit-22-4967),[21](#b21-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The exact role of each gene in MTX metabolism is not clear, and the interaction of each gene with FPGS makes it difficult to interpret the results we obtained. Second, a single time point was chosen in this study to reflect the system exposure extent of the drug, which might have had a large influence on the result precision when the plasma concentration was lower, which might be why no difference was found at 44 h. Third, the Kruskal-Wallis test was performed on the samples at 44 h because they were not in normal distribution (P=0.032) and the P value was equal to 0.055. Therefore, a large sample-size study was required to confirm the finding.

In this study we found higher efficacy of chemotherapy in patients with AA genotype than in those with GA and GG, further supported by the overall survival. The median survival time of patients with AA was significantly longer than in patients with other genotypes, with a significant difference of overall survival. Regarding the studies on MTX resistance, Stark et al. [[22](#b22-medscimonit-22-4967)] found impaired activity of FPGS in patients with MTX resistance. Further study in these patients demonstrated a defect in FPGS mRNA splicing, leading to premature termination and subsequent FPGS dysfunction and drug resistance. However, the exact mechanism by which this polymorphism affects FPGS function is not clear and requires further investigation.

## Conclusions

Our study demonstrated a correlation of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 with MTX treatment efficacy, as well as with overall survival, suggesting it could be used as an effective approach in the prediction of MTX treatment efficacy.

raw_tool_result.content:
1→# Effect of the Polymorphism of Folylpolyglutamate Synthetase on Treatment of High-Dose Methotrexate in Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia
     2→
     3→## Metadata
     4→**Authors:** Zhen Huang, Hong-Fei Tong, Yuan Li, Jiang-Chao Qian, Ju-Xiang Wang, Zhe Wang, Ji-Chen Ruan
     5→**Journal:** Medical Science Monitor : International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
     6→**Date:** 2016 Dec 17
     7→**DOI:** [10.12659/MSM.899021](https://doi.org/10.12659/MSM.899021)
     8→**PMID:** 27987364
     9→**PMCID:** PMC5189722
    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5189722/
    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf)
    12→
    13→## Abstract
    14→
    15→**Background:** 
    16→The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.
    17→
    18→**Material/Methods:** 
    19→57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.
    20→
    21→**Results:** 
    22→No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.
    23→
    24→**Conclusions:** 
    25→Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.
    26→
    27→MeSH Keywords: gamma-Glutamyl Hydrolase; Polymorphism, Single Nucleotide; Precursor Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia-Lymphoma
    28→
    29→### Background
    30→
    31→The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.
    32→
    33→### Material/Methods
    34→
    35→57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.
    36→
    37→### Results
    38→
    39→No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.
    40→
    41→### Conclusions
    42→
    43→Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.
    44→
    45→## Background
    46→
    47→Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is one of the most common hematologic malignancies in children. High-dose methotrexate (HD-MTX) has been shown to play an important role in the prophylaxis and treatment of extramedullary leukemia [[1](#b1-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Different treatment efficiency and adverse effects were observed in different individuals even with the same dose of MTX, suggesting the existence of differences in the MTX metabolism *in vivo* [[2](#b2-medscimonit-22-4967)]. As a catalase, folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) plays a critical role in the metabolism of MTX, and its deficiency can cause a dramatic reduction of intracellular MTX polyglutamate (MTXPG) level, leading to MTX resistance in leukemia cells [[3](#b3-medscimonit-22-4967),[4](#b4-medscimonit-22-4967)], which are further supported by the increased sensitivity of several glioma cells to MTX after overexpression of FPGS [[5](#b5-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In addition, a correlation of FPGS expression with intracellular MTXPG level and treatment outcome has been observed in pediatric patients with ALL [[6](#b6-medscimonit-22-4967),[7](#b7-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS rs1544105, resulting from C to T, has been identified as a functional polymorphism affecting FPGS activity in pediatric patients with ALL [[8](#b8-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Considering the important role of FPGS playing in MTX metabolism, whether FPGS’s function polymorphism rs1544105 affects the treatment of MTX in pediatric patients with ALL remains poorly understood. In this study, we aimed to analyze the genotype of rs1544105 in pediatric ALL patients and to conduct a preliminary investigation of the relationship between rs1544105 genotype with MTX plasma concentration or ALL prognosis.
    48→
    49→## Material and Methods
    50→
    51→### Patients
    52→
    53→From July 2012 to October 2014, 57 pediatric patients with newly diagnosed ALL were included in this study. The diagnosis of ALL was based on international guidelines [[9](#b9-medscimonit-22-4967)] and further confirmed by bone marrow cytology. All patients achieved remission after treatment with VDLP (Vincristine, Daunorubicin, L-asparaginase, and Prednisone). High-dose methotrexate was used for the prophylaxis of lymphocytic leukemia. Those patients were excluded from this study, including those who left the hospital or died before the end of 1 course of treatment, had impaired function of liver or kidney before chemotherapy, or who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Thirty-one age and sex-matched healthy children were served as a control group. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.
    54→
    55→### Reagents
    56→
    57→Methotrexate (batch no. HY21A) was purchased from Ruihui Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Reference substance for methotrexate (batch no. HWG00526) and levofloxacin (batch no. 130537-200301) were from the Chinese Institute for Drug Control.
    58→
    59→### Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism (PCR-RFLP)
    60→
    61→Genomic DNA was extracted from patients or control followed by PCR-RFLP analysis of the distribution of rs1544105 genotype, as previously described [[10](#b10-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Briefly, PCR amplification was performed with the following conditions: Initial denaturation (95°C for 10 min), 40 cycles of denaturation (95°C for 15 s), annealing (60°C for 1 min) and extension (72°C for 1 min), and final extension (72°C for 7 min). Sequences for PCR primers were: Forward: 5′-CCCAGAGTCCTTATTCTTAGCC-3′ and Reverse: 5′-GTGCCTCCTTCACACACAG-3′. After completion of amplification, restriction enzyme HpyCH4IV was added into the PCR product and incubated at 37°C for 4 h, followed by 2.5% agarose gel electrophoresis for analysis of RFLP.
    62→
    63→### Measurement of MTX concentration
    64→
    65→Peripheral venous blood was drawn from patients at 24 h or 44 h after MTX treatment, followed by measurement of MTX concentration by HPLC, as described previously [[11](#b11-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The conditions used for chromatographic analysis were as follows: stationary phase [ZORBAX XDB-C18 (4.6×250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA)], guard column [XDB-C18 (4.6×12.5 mm, 5 μm)], mobile phase [methyl cyanide /H_2_O: 30/70 (V/V), rate: 1 ml/min, column temperature: 30°C]. The detection wavelength was 225 nm.
    66→
    67→### Statistical analysis
    68→
    69→SPSS 19.0 software was used for statistical analysis. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium of genotype distribution as well as the difference of genotype in sex and risk group were evaluated by chi-square test. Comparison of MTX concentration within different genotypes was performed by one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test. Overall survival was assessed by Kaplan-Meier method. P<0.05 was considered to be a statistically significant difference.
    70→
    71→## Results
    72→
    73→### Patients’ characteristics
    74→
    75→This study included 57 pediatric ALL patients and 31 healthy children. No significant differences were observed in these 2 groups in sex or age. Detailed information is listed in [Table 1](#t1-medscimonit-22-4967).
    76→
    77→### Table 1.
    78→
    79→| Parameters | ALL group | The control group |
    80→| --- | --- | --- |
    81→| Age(years) | 5.9±4.3 | 3.7±1.6 |
    82→| Gender(n) |  |  |
    83→| Male | 31 | 18 |
    84→| Female | 26 | 13 |
    85→| ALL (n) |  |  |
    86→| T-Lineage | 11 |   |
    87→| B-Lineage | 46 |   |
    88→| Risk (n) |  |  |
    89→| Standard | 21 |   |
    90→| Intermediate | 27 |   |
    91→| High | 9 |   |
    92→| WBC (109 L) |  |  |
    93→| <50 | 52 |   |
    94→| ≥50 | 5 |   |
    95→
    96→Table 1 Caption: Clinical characteristics of patients with ALL and the control group.
    97→
    98→### Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105
    99→
   100→Polymorphism of rs1544105 was evaluated using the PCR-RFLP approach. After PCR amplification of FPGS, restricted enzyme HpyCH4IV was used to detect the mutation of rs1544105. After HpyCH4IV treatment on PCR product, 3 genotypes were detected based on the length after electrophoresis: homozygotes AA (298 bp), heterozygotes GA (298 bp, 168 bp, and 130 bp), and homozygotes GG (168 bp and 130 bp) ([Figure 1](#f1-medscimonit-22-4967)).
   101→
   102→### Figure 1.
   103→
   104→![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad94ef6b2a44/medscimonit-22-4967-g001.jpg)
   105→
   106→Electrophoresis of PCR-RFLP product. Genomic DNA was isolated from patients or controls, followed by analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 by PCR-RFLP. Lane 1 and 2: GA, Lane 3: GG, Lane 4: Marker, Lane 5 and 6: AA.
   107→
   108→### Genotype and alleles of FPGF rs1544105
   109→
   110→The genotype distribution of FPGS rs1544105 in ALL patients and healthy controls was in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (P>0.05). The distribution frequency of alleles was similar in these 2 groups. Compared to allele A, odds ratio (OR) of allele G was 0.97, with no significant difference ([Table 2](#t2-medscimonit-22-4967)).

   123→### Chromatographic analysis of MTX
   124→
   125→Chromatomaps for blank plasma ([Figure 2A](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), MTX plasma standard ([Figure 2B](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), and patient plasma ([Figure 2C](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)) were obtained through HPLC analysis ([Figure 2](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)). Chromatographic peak of MTX could be separated completely without obvious interference of endogenous impurity peak. The retention time for MTX and levofloxacin (internal standard) was 4.92 min and 9.42 min, respectively. A significant linear relation between MTX concentration and the ratio of MTX to internal standard peak area (Ai/As) was observed with regression equation being C=6.1328Ai/As – 0.0195 (r=0.9999) when MTX concentration was in 0.03–5.00 μg/ml, which was obtained from the standard curve under the current condition in this study. The lowest detected concentration was 0.03 μg/ml (RS, N=3). The recovery rate was 100.317±2.401%. Intra- and inter-batch errors as measured by precision test were 5.88% and 6.50%, respectively. MTX plasma samples were stable regardless of the storage condition, such as room temperature, frozen preservation, or freeze-thaw condition.

   135→### Relationship between polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 and MTX concentration
   136→
   137→In this study, we used dose-corrected MTX concentration per body surface area as an indicator to investigate the effect of polymorphism on MTX concentration. Our results showed that MTX C/D value (at 24 h) was significantly higher in ALL patients with AA genotype of rs1544105 than in patients with other genotypes (P<0.05) ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)). However, no difference in MTX C/D value was observed within different genotypes at 44 h ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

   152→### Association of rs1544105 polymorphism with prognosis
   153→
   154→The follow-up period in this study was from July 2012 to October 2014. Kaplan-Meier method was used for analysis of the association of rs1544105 polymorphism with survival time. Our results revealed a significant difference of overall survival between patients with AA genotype and GA + GG genotype (P<0.05) ([Table 4](#t4-medscimonit-22-4967)). After adjustment of white blood cell count, sex, age, and immune typing by Cox multivariate analysis, FPGS rs1544105 alleles were demonstrated to be an indicator for poor prognosis (log rank P=0.013, hazard ratio: 0.447, 95%CI: 0.237–0.842) ([Figure 3](#f3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

   174→
   175→## Discussion
   176→
   177→As an anti-tumor drug in the chemotherapy of pediatric ALL patients, methotrexate (MTX) plays an important role in the consolidation and maintenance treatment. In the clinic, plasma concentration of MTX and release of calcium folinate are monitored dynamically to reduce the occurrence of chemotherapy-induced adverse effects. A previous study demonstrated the existence of individual differences in the pharmacokinetics, effectiveness, and toxicity of MTX, suggesting there might be some potential gene targets affecting the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of MTX [[12](#b12-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS is an important enzyme in MTX metabolism, and catalyzed poly glutamic acid is the core of folate antagonists-associated cytotoxic therapy [[13](#b13-medscimonit-22-4967),[14](#b14-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Therefore, investigating the effect of FPGS polymorphism on drug treatment in ALL patients would have some important guiding significance [[15](#b15-medscimonit-22-4967)].
   178→
   179→Through analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105, we found that the genotype distribution in ALL patients and healthy controls were in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. The distribution frequencies of GG, GA, and AA genotype in healthy controls were close to HAPMap-HCB (Han Chinese in Beijing, China), which was 11.6%, 39.5%, and 48.8%, respectively [[16](#b16-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In this study we found the distribution frequency of alleles in ALL patients was close to that in controls, indicating no significant correlation of FPGS polymorphism with the pathogenesis of ALL, consistent with a previous study [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)].
   180→
   181→According to recommendations for ALL diagnosis and treatment, MTX doses for patients with low, medium, or high risk are different. To exclude the interference of different doses, dose-standardized MTX plasma concentration was used as an analysis indicator to monitor the dynamical concentration of MTX. Our results showed a significant difference in MTX concentration among patients with different genotypes at 24 h, showing a higher concentration for AA genotype than GA and GC (P<0.05). However, no difference was observed at 44 h. There are few reports on the relationship between FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism and the sensitivity and toxicity of MTX-associated chemotherapy, with contradicting results. Liu et al. [[18](#b18-medscimonit-22-4967)] and Panetta et al. [[19](#b19-medscimonit-22-4967)] demonstrated that FPGS activity was higher in individuals with GG than other genotypes with higher MTX plasma concentration as well as increased sensitivity to MTX. Sharma et al [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)]showed allele G might reduce the MTX treatment efficiency. In our study a difference of MTX concentration was found within different genotypes at 24 h but not 44 h. There may be several reasons to explain this. First, MTX metabolism is regulated by multiple genes, such as methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase, and γ-glutamyltransferase [[20](#b20-medscimonit-22-4967),[21](#b21-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The exact role of each gene in MTX metabolism is not clear, and the interaction of each gene with FPGS makes it difficult to interpret the results we obtained. Second, a single time point was chosen in this study to reflect the system exposure extent of the drug, which might have had a large influence on the result precision when the plasma concentration was lower, which might be why no difference was found at 44 h. Third, the Kruskal-Wallis test was performed on the samples at 44 h because they were not in normal distribution (P=0.032) and the P value was equal to 0.055. Therefore, a large sample-size study was required to confirm the finding.
   182→
   183→In this study we found higher efficacy of chemotherapy in patients with AA genotype than in those with GA and GG, further supported by the overall survival. The median survival time of patients with AA was significantly longer than in patients with other genotypes, with a significant difference of overall survival. Regarding the studies on MTX resistance, Stark et al. [[22](#b22-medscimonit-22-4967)] found impaired activity of FPGS in patients with MTX resistance. Further study in these patients demonstrated a defect in FPGS mRNA splicing, leading to premature termination and subsequent FPGS dysfunction and drug resistance. However, the exact mechanism by which this polymorphism affects FPGS function is not clear and requires further investigation.
   184→
   185→## Conclusions
   186→
   187→Our study demonstrated a correlation of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 with MTX treatment efficacy, as well as with overall survival, suggesting it could be used as an effective approach in the prediction of MTX treatment efficacy.

# Effect of the Polymorphism of Folylpolyglutamate Synthetase on Treatment of High-Dose Methotrexate in Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia

## Metadata
**Authors:** Zhen Huang, Hong-Fei Tong, Yuan Li, Jiang-Chao Qian, Ju-Xiang Wang, Zhe Wang, Ji-Chen Ruan
**Journal:** Medical Science Monitor : International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
**Date:** 2016 Dec 17
**DOI:** [10.12659/MSM.899021](https://doi.org/10.12659/MSM.899021)
**PMID:** 27987364
**PMCID:** PMC5189722
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5189722/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5189722/pdf/medscimonit-22-4967.pdf)

## Abstract

**Background:** 
The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.

**Material/Methods:** 
57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

**Results:** 
No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.

**Conclusions:** 
Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.

MeSH Keywords: gamma-Glutamyl Hydrolase; Polymorphism, Single Nucleotide; Precursor Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia-Lymphoma

### Background

The aim of this study was to investigate the association of the polymorphism of folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) with the dynamic plasma concentration of methotrexate (MTX) in pediatric patients with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), as well as the prognosis.

### Material/Methods

57 ALL patients and 31 age and sex-matched children (control) were included in this study. Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism was performed for the analysis of the genotype of FPGS rs1544105 and high-performance liquid chromatography for measurement of MTX plasma concentration after 24-h and 44-h treatment. Overall survival was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

### Results

No differences were observed between patients and controls regarding the distribution frequency of genotype and alleles of rs1544105. Patients carrying AA genotype had a significantly higher plasma concentration of MTX after 24 h than those carrying GG or GA (P<0.05) and no differences were found after 44 h. Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showed a longer median survival time in patients with AA than other genotypes with significant difference in overall survival.

### Conclusions

Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 might be used as an effective approach for prediction of the treatment outcome of MTX.

## Background

Acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL) is one of the most common hematologic malignancies in children. High-dose methotrexate (HD-MTX) has been shown to play an important role in the prophylaxis and treatment of extramedullary leukemia [[1](#b1-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Different treatment efficiency and adverse effects were observed in different individuals even with the same dose of MTX, suggesting the existence of differences in the MTX metabolism *in vivo* [[2](#b2-medscimonit-22-4967)]. As a catalase, folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS) plays a critical role in the metabolism of MTX, and its deficiency can cause a dramatic reduction of intracellular MTX polyglutamate (MTXPG) level, leading to MTX resistance in leukemia cells [[3](#b3-medscimonit-22-4967),[4](#b4-medscimonit-22-4967)], which are further supported by the increased sensitivity of several glioma cells to MTX after overexpression of FPGS [[5](#b5-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In addition, a correlation of FPGS expression with intracellular MTXPG level and treatment outcome has been observed in pediatric patients with ALL [[6](#b6-medscimonit-22-4967),[7](#b7-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS rs1544105, resulting from C to T, has been identified as a functional polymorphism affecting FPGS activity in pediatric patients with ALL [[8](#b8-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Considering the important role of FPGS playing in MTX metabolism, whether FPGS’s function polymorphism rs1544105 affects the treatment of MTX in pediatric patients with ALL remains poorly understood. In this study, we aimed to analyze the genotype of rs1544105 in pediatric ALL patients and to conduct a preliminary investigation of the relationship between rs1544105 genotype with MTX plasma concentration or ALL prognosis.

## Material and Methods

### Patients

From July 2012 to October 2014, 57 pediatric patients with newly diagnosed ALL were included in this study. The diagnosis of ALL was based on international guidelines [[9](#b9-medscimonit-22-4967)] and further confirmed by bone marrow cytology. All patients achieved remission after treatment with VDLP (Vincristine, Daunorubicin, L-asparaginase, and Prednisone). High-dose methotrexate was used for the prophylaxis of lymphocytic leukemia. Those patients were excluded from this study, including those who left the hospital or died before the end of 1 course of treatment, had impaired function of liver or kidney before chemotherapy, or who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Thirty-one age and sex-matched healthy children were served as a control group. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University. Informed consent was obtained from all participants.

### Reagents

Methotrexate (batch no. HY21A) was purchased from Ruihui Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Reference substance for methotrexate (batch no. HWG00526) and levofloxacin (batch no. 130537-200301) were from the Chinese Institute for Drug Control.

### Polymerase chain reaction-restriction fragment length polymorphism (PCR-RFLP)

Genomic DNA was extracted from patients or control followed by PCR-RFLP analysis of the distribution of rs1544105 genotype, as previously described [[10](#b10-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Briefly, PCR amplification was performed with the following conditions: Initial denaturation (95°C for 10 min), 40 cycles of denaturation (95°C for 15 s), annealing (60°C for 1 min) and extension (72°C for 1 min), and final extension (72°C for 7 min). Sequences for PCR primers were: Forward: 5′-CCCAGAGTCCTTATTCTTAGCC-3′ and Reverse: 5′-GTGCCTCCTTCACACACAG-3′. After completion of amplification, restriction enzyme HpyCH4IV was added into the PCR product and incubated at 37°C for 4 h, followed by 2.5% agarose gel electrophoresis for analysis of RFLP.

### Measurement of MTX concentration

Peripheral venous blood was drawn from patients at 24 h or 44 h after MTX treatment, followed by measurement of MTX concentration by HPLC, as described previously [[11](#b11-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The conditions used for chromatographic analysis were as follows: stationary phase [ZORBAX XDB-C18 (4.6×250 mm, 5 μm, Agilent, USA)], guard column [XDB-C18 (4.6×12.5 mm, 5 μm)], mobile phase [methyl cyanide /H_2_O: 30/70 (V/V), rate: 1 ml/min, column temperature: 30°C]. The detection wavelength was 225 nm.

### Statistical analysis

SPSS 19.0 software was used for statistical analysis. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium of genotype distribution as well as the difference of genotype in sex and risk group were evaluated by chi-square test. Comparison of MTX concentration within different genotypes was performed by one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test. Overall survival was assessed by Kaplan-Meier method. P<0.05 was considered to be a statistically significant difference.

## Results

### Patients’ characteristics

This study included 57 pediatric ALL patients and 31 healthy children. No significant differences were observed in these 2 groups in sex or age. Detailed information is listed in [Table 1](#t1-medscimonit-22-4967).

### Table 1.

| Parameters | ALL group | The control group |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Age(years) | 5.9±4.3 | 3.7±1.6 |
| Gender(n) |  |  |
| Male | 31 | 18 |
| Female | 26 | 13 |
| ALL (n) |  |  |
| T-Lineage | 11 |   |
| B-Lineage | 46 |   |
| Risk (n) |  |  |
| Standard | 21 |   |
| Intermediate | 27 |   |
| High | 9 |   |
| WBC (109 L) |  |  |
| <50 | 52 |   |
| ≥50 | 5 |   |

Table 1 Caption: Clinical characteristics of patients with ALL and the control group.

### Polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105

Polymorphism of rs1544105 was evaluated using the PCR-RFLP approach. After PCR amplification of FPGS, restricted enzyme HpyCH4IV was used to detect the mutation of rs1544105. After HpyCH4IV treatment on PCR product, 3 genotypes were detected based on the length after electrophoresis: homozygotes AA (298 bp), heterozygotes GA (298 bp, 168 bp, and 130 bp), and homozygotes GG (168 bp and 130 bp) ([Figure 1](#f1-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Figure 1.

![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad94ef6b2a44/medscimonit-22-4967-g001.jpg)

Electrophoresis of PCR-RFLP product. Genomic DNA was isolated from patients or controls, followed by analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 by PCR-RFLP. Lane 1 and 2: GA, Lane 3: GG, Lane 4: Marker, Lane 5 and 6: AA.

### Genotype and alleles of FPGF rs1544105

The genotype distribution of FPGS rs1544105 in ALL patients and healthy controls was in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (P>0.05). The distribution frequency of alleles was similar in these 2 groups. Compared to allele A, odds ratio (OR) of allele G was 0.97, with no significant difference ([Table 2](#t2-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 2.

|   | Genotype (%) |  |  | Gene frequency |  | OR | 95%CI |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| GG | GA | AA | G | A |  |  |  |
| ALL (n=57) | 11 (19.30%) | 21 (36.84%) | 25 (43.86%) | 43 (37.72%) | 71 (62.28%) | 0.965 | 0.764–1.219 |
| Control (n=31) | 4 (12.90%) | 14 (45.16%) | 13 (41.94%) | 22 (35.48%) | 40 (64.52%) | 1 |   |
| P | 0.654a |  |  | 0.769b |  |   |   |

Table 2 Caption: Distribution of FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism in ALL and control.

### Chromatographic analysis of MTX

Chromatomaps for blank plasma ([Figure 2A](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), MTX plasma standard ([Figure 2B](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)), and patient plasma ([Figure 2C](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)) were obtained through HPLC analysis ([Figure 2](#f2-medscimonit-22-4967)). Chromatographic peak of MTX could be separated completely without obvious interference of endogenous impurity peak. The retention time for MTX and levofloxacin (internal standard) was 4.92 min and 9.42 min, respectively. A significant linear relation between MTX concentration and the ratio of MTX to internal standard peak area (Ai/As) was observed with regression equation being C=6.1328Ai/As – 0.0195 (r=0.9999) when MTX concentration was in 0.03–5.00 μg/ml, which was obtained from the standard curve under the current condition in this study. The lowest detected concentration was 0.03 μg/ml (RS, N=3). The recovery rate was 100.317±2.401%. Intra- and inter-batch errors as measured by precision test were 5.88% and 6.50%, respectively. MTX plasma samples were stable regardless of the storage condition, such as room temperature, frozen preservation, or freeze-thaw condition.

### Figure 2.

![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/13a08c3247ee/medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g002.jpg)

HPLC analysis of the plasma concentration of MTX. Through using blank plasma (A) and MTX plasma standard (B), plasma concentration of MTX (C) after administration was measured by HPLC.

### Relationship between polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 and MTX concentration

In this study, we used dose-corrected MTX concentration per body surface area as an indicator to investigate the effect of polymorphism on MTX concentration. Our results showed that MTX C/D value (at 24 h) was significantly higher in ALL patients with AA genotype of rs1544105 than in patients with other genotypes (P<0.05) ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)). However, no difference in MTX C/D value was observed within different genotypes at 44 h ([Table 3](#t3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 3.

| Time after MTX infusion | FPGS genotype | C/D (mmol/L per g/m2) | F | P |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 24 hour | AA | 15.30±2.98 | 4.125 | 0.023a |
| AG | 14.14±3.19 |  |  |  |
| GG | 12.19±2.59 |  |  |  |
| 44 hour | AA | 0.11±0.04 | 3.065 | 0.055b |
| AG | 0.11±0.05 |  |  |  |
| GG | 0.08±0.03 |  |  |  |

Table 3 Caption: Relationship between FPGS genotype and concentration of MTX.

### Association of rs1544105 polymorphism with prognosis

The follow-up period in this study was from July 2012 to October 2014. Kaplan-Meier method was used for analysis of the association of rs1544105 polymorphism with survival time. Our results revealed a significant difference of overall survival between patients with AA genotype and GA + GG genotype (P<0.05) ([Table 4](#t4-medscimonit-22-4967)). After adjustment of white blood cell count, sex, age, and immune typing by Cox multivariate analysis, FPGS rs1544105 alleles were demonstrated to be an indicator for poor prognosis (log rank P=0.013, hazard ratio: 0.447, 95%CI: 0.237–0.842) ([Figure 3](#f3-medscimonit-22-4967)).

### Table 4.

| Group | Median survival time (w) |  | 95% Confidence Interval (w) |  | F | P |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Estimate | Std Error | Lower bound | Upper bound |  |  |  |
| Overall | 37.400 | 6.912 | 23.853 | 50.947 |   |   |
| AA | 52.000 | 12.079 | 28.326 | 75.674 |   |   |
| AG+GG | 35.000 | 4.011 | 27.139 | 42.861 | 4.352 | 0.037a |

Table 4 Caption: Overall survival analysis in ALL patients with different FPGS genotypes.

### Figure 3.

![Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb7f/5189722/ad233f47ed4e/medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)

[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=5189722_medscimonit-22-4967-g003.jpg)

Analysis of overall survival and Cox regression in ALL patients with different genotypes. Overall survival in patients treated with MTX was analyzed by Kaplan-Meier method.

## Discussion

As an anti-tumor drug in the chemotherapy of pediatric ALL patients, methotrexate (MTX) plays an important role in the consolidation and maintenance treatment. In the clinic, plasma concentration of MTX and release of calcium folinate are monitored dynamically to reduce the occurrence of chemotherapy-induced adverse effects. A previous study demonstrated the existence of individual differences in the pharmacokinetics, effectiveness, and toxicity of MTX, suggesting there might be some potential gene targets affecting the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of MTX [[12](#b12-medscimonit-22-4967)]. FPGS is an important enzyme in MTX metabolism, and catalyzed poly glutamic acid is the core of folate antagonists-associated cytotoxic therapy [[13](#b13-medscimonit-22-4967),[14](#b14-medscimonit-22-4967)]. Therefore, investigating the effect of FPGS polymorphism on drug treatment in ALL patients would have some important guiding significance [[15](#b15-medscimonit-22-4967)].

Through analysis of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105, we found that the genotype distribution in ALL patients and healthy controls were in accordance with Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. The distribution frequencies of GG, GA, and AA genotype in healthy controls were close to HAPMap-HCB (Han Chinese in Beijing, China), which was 11.6%, 39.5%, and 48.8%, respectively [[16](#b16-medscimonit-22-4967)]. In this study we found the distribution frequency of alleles in ALL patients was close to that in controls, indicating no significant correlation of FPGS polymorphism with the pathogenesis of ALL, consistent with a previous study [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)].

According to recommendations for ALL diagnosis and treatment, MTX doses for patients with low, medium, or high risk are different. To exclude the interference of different doses, dose-standardized MTX plasma concentration was used as an analysis indicator to monitor the dynamical concentration of MTX. Our results showed a significant difference in MTX concentration among patients with different genotypes at 24 h, showing a higher concentration for AA genotype than GA and GC (P<0.05). However, no difference was observed at 44 h. There are few reports on the relationship between FPGS rs1544105 polymorphism and the sensitivity and toxicity of MTX-associated chemotherapy, with contradicting results. Liu et al. [[18](#b18-medscimonit-22-4967)] and Panetta et al. [[19](#b19-medscimonit-22-4967)] demonstrated that FPGS activity was higher in individuals with GG than other genotypes with higher MTX plasma concentration as well as increased sensitivity to MTX. Sharma et al [[17](#b17-medscimonit-22-4967)]showed allele G might reduce the MTX treatment efficiency. In our study a difference of MTX concentration was found within different genotypes at 24 h but not 44 h. There may be several reasons to explain this. First, MTX metabolism is regulated by multiple genes, such as methylene tetrahydrofolate reductase, and γ-glutamyltransferase [[20](#b20-medscimonit-22-4967),[21](#b21-medscimonit-22-4967)]. The exact role of each gene in MTX metabolism is not clear, and the interaction of each gene with FPGS makes it difficult to interpret the results we obtained. Second, a single time point was chosen in this study to reflect the system exposure extent of the drug, which might have had a large influence on the result precision when the plasma concentration was lower, which might be why no difference was found at 44 h. Third, the Kruskal-Wallis test was performed on the samples at 44 h because they were not in normal distribution (P=0.032) and the P value was equal to 0.055. Therefore, a large sample-size study was required to confirm the finding.

In this study we found higher efficacy of chemotherapy in patients with AA genotype than in those with GA and GG, further supported by the overall survival. The median survival time of patients with AA was significantly longer than in patients with other genotypes, with a significant difference of overall survival. Regarding the studies on MTX resistance, Stark et al. [[22](#b22-medscimonit-22-4967)] found impaired activity of FPGS in patients with MTX resistance. Further study in these patients demonstrated a defect in FPGS mRNA splicing, leading to premature termination and subsequent FPGS dysfunction and drug resistance. However, the exact mechanism by which this polymorphism affects FPGS function is not clear and requires further investigation.

## Conclusions

Our study demonstrated a correlation of the polymorphism of FPGS rs1544105 with MTX treatment efficacy, as well as with overall survival, suggesting it could be used as an effective approach in the prediction of MTX treatment efficacy.

## Footnotes

## References

Pharmacotherapy. 2005;25:748–55. doi: 10.1592/phco.25.5.748.63584. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1038/leu.2008.66) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18368069/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Leukemia&title=Polymorphisms%20in%20folate-related%20genes:%20Association%20with%20side%20effects%20of%20high-dose%20methotrexate%20in%20childhood%20acute%20lymphoblastic%20leukemia&author=L%20Huang&author=WJ%20Tissing&author=R%20de%20Jonge&volume=22&publication_year=2008&pages=1798-800&pmid=18368069&doi=10.1038/leu.2008.66&)
